lines.text: Against Me!, 'Sink, Florida, Sink.' New Wave album, 2007. We're {{ELAPSED}} in and the body count is worse than last year at this point. You know what that means.

Laura Jane Grace. Founded the band in Naples in '97, moved to Gainesville that same year. Said the name reflected how she felt — 'The whole world is against me.' Fourteen years later, still feels right.
This song — it's about drowning. About sinking. Grace doesn't write metaphors. She writes what she means. And she means it.
New Wave hit number 57 on the Billboard 200. Nothing punk was supposed to chart that high. They did it anyway. That's the Against Me! move — say no to everything and chart anyway.
Play it loud. The clock reads {{CLOCK}}. Three hours until sunrise. Three hours until the bodies stop moving.
The Bouncing Souls. 'Ole.' 1999. From Hopeless Romantic. New Brunswick, New Jersey. Best kept secret in punk rock for thirty years.
Greg Attonito, Pete Steinkopf, Bryan Kienlen — they met in Basking Ridge high school, moved to New Brunswick together. Built a scene from nothing. Called their fans 'True Believers' and meant it.
This song right here — 'Ole' — it's a singalong. At 4am on Purge night, you need a singalong. You need to remember that people are still alive out there, still listening, still here.
The Souls built Chunksaah Records themselves. Started with nothing. Founded their own label. That's the math of punk — you don't wait for permission, you build it yourself or you don't build.

There's a story — one of the members was homeless, wrote 'Gone' sitting on a street corner. Heard a song in a parked car that gave him hope. Wanted to write something that would do the same for someone else. That's punk rock. That's what it's supposed to be.
Hot Water Music. 'A Good Chance.' From Caution, 2002. Gainesville, Florida. Chuck Ragan, Chris Wollard, Jason Black, George Rebelo.

The band's name came from Charles Bukowski — a collection of short stories called Hot Water Music. Four guys frustrated by nothing happening in their hometown, Black convinced them to move to Gainesville. Built something real there.

Dual vocals — Ragan and Wollard. You hear it on this track. Call and response. Like they're arguing with themselves about whether survival is even worth the effort. By the bridge, they've decided it is.

They nearly broke up in '98 from touring exhaustion. Were actually done. Then they rehearsed the farewell show and wrote four new songs in rehearsal. The gig itself convinced them to keep going. Almost lost Hot Water Music to burnout. Almost.
The lyrics are all working-class resilience. Personal struggle. Emotional vulnerability. This isn't macho punk. This is punk that admits it's scared.

tracks.title: Sunday Morning
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Sunday Morning.' 1997. Have a Ball album. A cover band from San Francisco that became the most important cover band in punk rock history.

The lineup — Fat Mike from NOFX, Joey Cape from Lagwagon, Chris Shiflett who'd go on to Foo Fighters, Spike Slawson from Swingin' Utters. All of them formed this thing just to cover songs when their main bands had downtime.

Have a Ball was 1960s and '70s pop covers done at punk speed. Converted saccharine hits into something raw. That's the Gimmes move — take what's mainstream, strip it down, play it like your life depends on it.
They wore matching costumes on stage. Quirky. Weird. Committed to the bit. That's punk rock commitment — if you're gonna do it, own the ridiculousness.
This track — it's tender. Three hours from sunrise. We're playing the tender ones now.
NOFX. 'The Decline.' 1996. Eighteen minutes on a single track. All of it one continuous middle finger aimed at everything.

Fat Mike, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin — they formed in Los Angeles in '83, took the name from a Boston hardcore band called Negative FX. Three best friends from Fairfax High. Stayed friends for forty years.
El Hefe joined in '91 on lead guitar and trumpet. That's when NOFX became NOFX. The sound locked in. The politics sharpened. The cynicism got surgical.

Punk in Drublic in '94 was their moment. Hit number twelve on the Heatseekers chart. Got gold status six years later. Punk radio wasn't supposed to chart. They didn't care what wasn't supposed to happen.
'The Decline' is fiery social commentary. Dub reggae sections, spoken word, pure noise, back to punk. Eighteen minutes of saying 'no' to everything and meaning it every second.

Fat Mike said something about the 'Leave It Alone' video from Punk in Drublic — said they didn't even send it to MTV. Didn't want to be part of the machine. That's the NOFX principle. You don't negotiate with the beast. You mock it from outside.
The Lawrence Arms. 'Coming Home.' The Greatest Story Ever Told, 2003. Chicago. Brendan Kelly, Chris McCaughan, Neil Hennessy.

These three met in Chicago, formed the band in '99, named it after an apartment complex they got evicted from in the middle of the night. That's the Lawrence Arms story — built something from nothing, lost it, built it again.

Fat Mike signed them to Fat Wreck Chords in 2001. They went on Warped Tour in 2002, got booted for publicly calling out the organizers for selling bottled water for more than five bucks. Five dollars. That was the price of punk rock integrity.

Brendan Kelly said something that matters — said they write about experiences influenced by alcohol, good ones and terrible ones. That's the Lawrence Arms lens. Dark humor. Personal mythology. Truth that hurts.

The Greatest Story Ever Told had footnotes. Actual footnotes in the CD liner notes explaining references to history and literature and pop culture woven into the lyrics. Punk rock that does its homework.
'Coming Home' is what you play when you're three hours from sunrise and you realize you made it through another night. It's acknowledgment. It's gratitude. It's the song that says 'I'm still here.'

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide cumulative: 165 kills. {{ELAPSED}} elapsed. East Side holding steady at 18% of total. Numbers tracking +9% versus 2168 at this hour.
MorningCare Triage: Open until sunrise. Response time guarantee: 24 minutes or less. Call 1-800-MORNING. Post-Purge medical. Discrete. Professional.
Lake Mendota: Coast Guard volunteers resuming shoreline sweep at 05:30. One unrecovered, presumed deceased. Fog lifting. Visibility improving.
